text.title: Hook Model and Retention Loop Types
text.summary: The Hook model (Trigger → Action → Variable Reward → Investment) maps each product flow to concrete retention mechanics; retention loop type must be identified before applying the Hook model.

section.title: Retention Loop Types
section.p: A retention loop is a self-reinforcing cycle where using the product creates reasons to return. Identify the primary type before designing any retention mechanic.

list.item: Content loop: new content from creators or algorithm draws users back (Netflix, TikTok)
Social loop: activity from connections motivates return (Facebook, Slack)
Progress loop: accumulating value makes leaving costly (Duolingo streaks)
Stored value loop: data or content the user created lives only here (Notion, Evernote)
Habit loop: triggered by a recurring routine (morning news app)
Network loop: others depend on the user's presence (team collaboration tools)

rule.statement: Define the trigger first; all other Hook components depend on it being reliable.
rule.rationale: Without a trigger the action never starts. External triggers (push, email) are controllable but decay; internal triggers (boredom, loneliness, uncertainty) are durable but must be cultivated by associating the product with an existing emotional state.

rule.statement: Reduce the action to the minimum possible behavior between trigger and reward.
rule.rationale: Every extra step between trigger and reward is friction that causes drop-off. The action should be the simplest behavior in anticipation of the reward — one tap, one swipe, one scan.

rule.statement: Rewards must be variable; predictable rewards become boring and stop driving return.
rule.rationale: Variable schedules of reinforcement (unpredictable reward magnitude or timing) sustain engagement longer than fixed schedules. Three reward types: Tribe (social validation — likes, comments), Hunt (searching for content — scrolling), Self (personal achievement — level-up, streak).

rule.statement: Each Investment must increase future value and load the next trigger.
rule.rationale: Investment creates stored value (data, preferences, social ties, reputation) that raises the cost of switching and makes the next session more valuable. Examples: following accounts, completing a profile, creating content, learning a shortcut.

rule.statement: Taper external triggers over time; the goal is internal trigger formation.
rule.rationale: External triggers (email, push) have open-rate decay and fatigue risk. The lifecycle plan should reduce trigger frequency as internal triggers form — measure internal trigger adoption by tracking unprompted direct opens vs notification-driven opens.

list.item: Best: internal trigger (user thinks of you when stimulus X occurs)
Good: event-based trigger (real activity to show — "John commented")
Weak: generic reminder with no contextual anchor

antipattern.description: Applying streak mechanics to work tools or finance products.
antipattern.reason: Streaks work for habit/learning categories but backfire in contexts where users want predictability. Breaking a streak punishes power users who take vacations. Provide streak-freeze mechanics or choose a different Investment type.

antipattern.description: Using variable reward to drive engagement through anxiety triggers (infinite scroll, fear-of-missing-out notifications).
antipattern.reason: Manipulative designs face regulatory risk (EU DSA, UK Online Safety Act, AU eSafety) and destroy long-term trust. Variable reward must be net-positive in expectation; users who feel cheated uninstall and leave negative reviews.

antipattern.description: Measuring retention with DAU/MAU for all products regardless of use-case cadence.
antipattern.reason: DAU/MAU is appropriate for habit products (daily news, social). For tools (weekly), utilities (monthly), or B2B workflows, the metric is misleading. Define "active" operationally per product type before measuring.

text.reference: Fill-in-the-blank Hook sentence template per product flow
